data.title: Select Medical outlines $5.6B-$5.8B 2026 revenue outlook while planning 275 additional beds through 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Select Medical (SEM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “On March 2, we announced that Select Medical entered into an agreement to be acquired by a consortium led by our Executive Chairman, Robert Ortenzio, together with Martin Jackson and Welsh, Carson, Anderson & Stowe,” said (Chief Executive Officer Thomas Mullin). “Under the terms of the agreement, unaffiliated shareholders will receive $16.50 per share in cash,” and management said it “expect[s] it to close in mid-2026,” noting the Hart-Scott-Rodino waiting period “expired on April 27.”

  • “In connection with and contingent upon the completion of the transaction, our senior secured credit facilities will provide for an additional $1 billion of term loan borrowings, bearing interest at a rate equal to SOFR plus 3%,” said (CEO Mullin).

  • “So far this year, we’ve added 166 beds across 3 newly opened inpatient rehabilitation hospitals,” said (CEO Mullin), and added, “Across the remainder of 2026 and into 2027, we expect to add 275 more beds, 209 will be in IRF and 66 in critical illness.”

  • “All 3 of our operating divisions delivered revenue growth versus the prior year period, with total revenue increasing by 5% overall,” said (CEO Mullin). “Adjusted EBITDA declined 6.5% to $141.6 million,” and “earnings per common share was $0.35,” while “adjusted for the take-private transaction costs,” EPS “was $0.36.”

  • “At the end of the quarter, we had $1.9 billion of total debt outstanding and $25.7 million of cash on the balance sheet,” said (Executive VP & CFO Michael Malatesta). “We are maintaining our full year 2026 guidance.”

Outlook

  • “We are maintaining our full year 2026 guidance,” said (CFO Malatesta). “We continue to expect revenue to range between $5.6 billion and $5.8 billion and adjusted EBITDA between $520 million and $540 million,” and “fully diluted earnings per common share is expected to be in the range of $1.22 to $1.32.”

  • Management’s guidance language was unchanged from the prior quarter’s outlook issuance for 2026, when the company also guided to revenue of $5.6 billion to $5.8 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $520 million to $540 million, and fully diluted EPS of $1.22 to $1.32.

Financial results

  • The company reported consolidated “adjusted EBITDA declined 6.5% to $141.6 million,” said (CEO Mullin), alongside total revenue growth of 5% and EPS of $0.35 (or $0.36 excluding take-private costs).

  • In inpatient rehabilitation, “revenue increased more than 14% year-over-year to approximately $351.9 million,” and “adjusted EBITDA increased 15% to $81.1 million,” said (CEO Mullin), adding that “average daily census grew 12%” and “same-store occupancy increased to 87% from 83%.”

  • In critical illness recovery hospitals, “adjusted EBITDA declined 15% to $73.4 million,” and management attributed pressure in part to Medicare Advantage dynamics discussed in Q&A.

  • In outpatient rehabilitation, “adjusted EBITDA was $22 million,” and management tied performance to operational actions and market exits discussed in Q&A.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC Capital Markets: asked about outpatient rehab margin improvement and sustainability; CEO Mullin: “We’ve been doing a lot around scheduling and schedule optimization,” and “if we do not see a path out, we’re going to be exiting those markets,” adding, “that was approximately $1 million of cost that flowed through in the first quarter… Oregon, where we closed 4 clinics.”

  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC Capital Markets: asked for an update on broader policy efforts around the LTACH high-cost outlier; CEO Mullin: “we are encouraged” the proposal keeps the threshold consistent and said preliminary data had the company “running at or below that threshold… of 7.975% of Medicare revenue being in the outlier bucket,” while outlining a future advocacy focus to “pivot to more of the patients” excluded by prior LTAC criteria.

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: asked whether the company is seeing increased denials, including from Medicare Advantage; CEO Mullin: “We did see an increase… a decrease in conversion for Medicare Advantage,” adding, “we’re seeing more denials in the Medicare Advantage space for our hospitals,” while “in outpatient, it’s been relatively flat.”

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: asked whether anything in the inpatient rehab proposed rule was surprising; CEO Mullin: “there were no concerns with the rule… it was pretty benign and nothing out of the ordinary.”

  • Joaquin Eduardo Martinez (for Joanna Gajuk), BofA Securities: asked about CIRH gross margins and whether management expects recovery; CFO Malatesta: Medicare Advantage conversion declines “impacted our volume,” with “approximately $13 million to $14 million” year-over-year impact, and added, “we do expect to still be within our expectations for the remainder of the year.”

  • Joaquin Eduardo Martinez (for Joanna Gajuk), BofA Securities: asked for early read on the Medicare TEAM Model; CFO Malatesta: “we haven’t really seen an impact to our census in the inpatient rehab space,” and CEO Mullin: “Everything that we’ve seen so far is a very minor issue in our rehab hospitals.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on reimbursement risk and friction points (denials and high-cost outlier) and profitability (outpatient margin, CIRH margins), including “increase in… denials” (Mizuho) and “gross margins in the CIRH segment” (BofA).

  • Management tone was neutral to slightly positive, emphasizing development momentum and stability in guidance; management used reassurance framing such as “we are maintaining our full year 2026 guidance” (CFO Malatesta) and “there were no concerns with the rule” (CEO Mullin).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the call shifted from process uncertainty (“nonbinding indication of interest” under review) to transaction specificity (cash price, timing expectations, HSR expiration) and more pointed Q&A on Medicare Advantage denials and CIRH pressure.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest update was governance/strategy: Q4 described a “nonbinding indication of interest” under special committee review, while Q1 detailed a signed take-private agreement at $16.50 per share, an expected mid-2026 close, and incremental $1 billion term loan capacity contingent on completion.

  • Operational emphasis remained consistent on inpatient rehab expansion, with Q1 updating bed openings and a refined forward pipeline (“expect to add 275 more beds” across 2026-2027) compared with Q4’s broader “expect to add 399 beds” across 2026-2027.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s heavy attention on outpatient margin drivers (health insurance expense, variable discounts, payer mix) to Q1’s focus on outpatient operational fixes and exits, plus Medicare Advantage denials and CIRH volume/margin sensitivity.

Risks and concerns

  • Management said Medicare Advantage pressure was evident: “We’re seeing more denials in the Medicare Advantage space for our hospitals,” said (CEO Mullin), and CFO Malatesta quantified a year-over-year impact in critical illness of “approximately $13 million to $14 million.”

  • The company highlighted execution risk in outpatient footprint optimization: CEO Mullin said, “if we do not see a path out, we’re going to be exiting those markets,” and noted clinic closures in Oregon.

  • Regulatory reimbursement remains a key swing factor, with management pointing to proposed CMS rate updates for FY 2027 and monitoring high-cost outlier mechanics; CEO Mullin said the company is “encouraged” by the proposed stability and expects to engage in “lobbying efforts and the conversations with CMS.”

Final takeaway

Management centered Q1 on the signed take-private deal (including a $16.50 per share cash price, an expected mid-2026 close, and contingent $1 billion of additional term-loan capacity) alongside continued inpatient rehabilitation expansion and unchanged full-year 2026 guidance. In Q&A, analysts pressed on outpatient margins, Medicare Advantage denials, and critical-illness profitability, with management pointing to scheduling productivity work, planned market exits where performance does not improve, and reported Medicare Advantage conversion declines as a driver of critical-illness pressure, while characterizing the TEAM model impact as limited so far.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Japan stocks see biggest weekly inflow since 2013 as global investors return: BofA
data.description:
Japan's equity markets are attracting their biggest wave of foreign capital in over a decade, with BofA Global Research and EPFR data showing the largest weekly inflow into Japanese stocks since May 2013, a milestone that stands out even more starkly given the turbulent outflow environment that preceded it.

The chart tells a dramatic story of reversal. After a period of sustained and at times severe weekly outflows through late 2025 and early 2026, including a notable trough that saw outflows approach nearly $12 billion in a single week, capital has swung sharply back into Japan in recent weeks.

The most recent data point, circled prominently in the BofA chart, shows weekly inflows breaching $6 billion, matching levels last seen during the Abenomics-driven euphoria of 2013 when Prime Minister Shinzo Abe's bold stimulus policies first drew a flood of global money into Japanese equities.

The 4-week moving average, which smooths out the weekly volatility, has also turned decisively positive after spending much of the past year in negative territory, a sign that the renewed interest in Japan is not a one-week anomaly but a building trend.

The shift in flows is being driven by a combination of factors: a weaker yen making Japanese exporters more competitive and attractive to foreign investors, continued corporate governance reforms pushing companies to improve shareholder returns, and a broader global rotation away from U.S. assets as investors seek diversification amid ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainty.

For context, the scale of recent outflows that preceded this reversal was historically severe, the drawdowns of late 2025 were among the deepest on the entire 14-year chart, suggesting the current inflow surge may partly reflect a sharp mean reversion as investors who fled during the crisis period return to rebuild Japan exposure.

Whether the momentum is sustained will likely depend on the trajectory of the yen, the Bank of Japan's policy path, and how long global investors remain willing to look beyond U.S. markets for returns.

data.title: Coffee buzz: Dutch Bros lands a bull rating from Oppenheimer
data.description:
Oppenheimer started off coverage on Dutch Bros (BROS) with an Outperform rating.

Analyst Brian Bittner said the restaurant stock represents a unique opportunity to own an "on-trend" beverage brand with robust traffic drivers, mid-teens unit growth, and an EBITDA algorithm of more than 20% growth. Bittner noted that the company's track record of healthy same-store sales growth has runway with upside expected out to 2027 as company-specific catalysts take hold.

Importantly, Bittner and his team believe investor concerns about competition risks are overblown. "We're fans of the long-term compounding opportunity, but also actionable on the stock's 2026 setup as we look toward 1Q26 results (on 5/6)—particularly with near-term coffee cost headwinds conservatively in '26E forecasts," he wrote.

Oppenheimer assigned a price target of $72 to BROS.

data.title: Fannie Mae downgraded at Wedbush after Q1 earnings over no IPO news
data.description:
Fannie Mae (FNMA) was downgraded at Wedbush Securities after first-quarter earnings, with no mention or news on IPO plans.

The residential mortgage financier's Q1 net interest income fell short of analyst expectations, but revenue surpassed consensus.

"FNMA reported Q1 earnings results that were modestly below our expectations, with the headline story being expense discipline, where the administrative expense ratio declined to 10.2% from 14.0% just four quarters ago, reflecting aggressive workforce and real estate footprint reductions," said analysts Michael Piccolo and Henry Coffey.

"Single-family was the standout segment on the highest acquisition volume since 2022, driven by a refi surge, and multifamily was the key risk as delinquencies rose, which marks five consecutive quarters of deterioration," said the analysts in a research note.

"While the political momentum towards an IPO or recap & release has arguably stalled, in our view, until after the midterms, the company is making favorable progress at improving efficiency and building capital," said the note.

"Again, there was no mention or news on IPO plans or recap & release. Given shares of FNMA are now trading above our $8 per share price target, we are downgrading our rating to Neutral (from Outperform) on valuation," added the research note.

Wedbush's rating aligns with Seeking Alpha authors as well as the Quant rating of Hold. Meanwhile, the Wall Street analysts grade the stock as Buy.

data.title: Vista Gold outlines permit approvals through 2027 as it targets detailed engineering start in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Vista Gold (VGZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “During the first quarter, we completed a public offering for aggregate gross proceeds of $44.85 million and net proceeds of $42 million,” said President, CEO & Director Frederick H. Earnest, adding that 2026 priorities include “all activities leading to permit modification approvals to align the existing permits with the 2025 feasibility study designs,” “completing predevelopment optimizations,” “building an Australian-based team to lead project development,” and “advancing project execution planning.”
  • “We ended the first quarter with a strong balance sheet,” said Chief Financial Officer Douglas Tobler, specifying “cash on hand of $52.7 million compared to a cash balance of $13.6 million at year-end 2025,” and adding, “we also continue to have a clean balance sheet with no debt at March 31, 2026.”
  • “In January, we appointed Dr. Francis Kuranchie as our approvals manager based in Darwin,” said CEO Earnest, describing a multi-step approvals path that includes “approval is expected early in Q3” for certain approvals under review, while “the final or last authorization, under the Environmental Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act [is] expected sometime in the middle or latter part of 2027.”

Outlook

  • “The results that we're achieving are being completed efficiently and support our target of commencing detailed engineering and design in 2027,” said CFO Tobler.
  • “Completing this work along with the permit modifications are important prerequisites to initiating detailed engineering design, which we expect to begin in 2027,” said CEO Earnest, who also tied the decision point to permitting, stating the company is working “to manage the approvals process to achieve final results in the most expedient manner possible.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing, management again emphasized a 2027 engineering start, with CEO Earnest previously stating, “Completing this work is an important prerequisite to initiating detailed engineering and design, which we expect to begin in 2027.”

Financial Results

  • “We reported a net loss of $3.1 million for the 3 months ended March 31, 2026,” said CFO Tobler, comparing it to “a net loss of $2.7 million for the 3 months ended March 31, 2025.”
  • CFO Tobler attributed the higher loss to operating cost drivers, saying, “Mt. Todd project related recurring costs increased by about $100,000 due to addition of project management team members and higher power costs due to water management pumping requirements,” while “corporate expenses… increased about $300,000… from additional legal, consulting and board costs associated with ongoing advancement of Mt. Todd.”
  • On funding capacity post-offering, CFO Tobler said the financing “provides us with the ability to move forward immediately with key programs and add the appropriate resources to our organization to support the beginning of detailed engineering.”

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was not observable in the current quarter because the operator stated, “We have no questions.”
  • Management sentiment in prepared remarks was confident and promotional, with CEO Earnest stating, “we are confident that approvals will be granted,” and arguing Mt Todd “holds tremendous intrinsic value,” while also citing market uncertainty: “We've seen a lot of volatility in the gold price since the first of the year.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q4 included more investor scrutiny in Q&A (financing and cost inflation), while Q1’s tone stayed largely one-directional, with CEO Earnest reiterating, “we now have the financial resources to successfully complete the tasks of obtaining final project authorizations.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current quarter shifted emphasis from announcing the equity raise to deploying it: CEO Earnest said, “we completed a public offering,” and CFO Tobler said the funding “provides us with the ability to move forward immediately with key programs,” while Q4 framed it as recently completed, with CFO Tobler saying Vista “has recently completed an equity offering with net proceeds of $41.9 million.”
  • The permitting narrative became more date-specific in Q1, including “approval is expected early in Q3” for approvals under review and an EPBC timing expectation “in the middle or latter part of 2027,” while Q4 described the process more generally: “Applications were submitted for several modifications with additional applications planned for this year.”
  • Operational execution details expanded in Q1 around work programs and staffing: CEO Earnest said “metallurgical testing is underway at ALS Amtech in Perth” and “we have hired 4 senior project management team members based in Perth,” while Q4 highlighted the study foundation and early-stage hiring announcements.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Earnest highlighted permitting sequencing risk, saying an EPA referral filing is expected “once we have clarity on the new Aboriginal Areas Protection Authority authorization,” and he flagged the long-dated nature of final federal authorization: “the final or last authorization… expected sometime in the middle or latter part of 2027.”
  • Weather and site-water management featured as an operational risk, with CEO Earnest noting “the most severe wet season in 20 years with approximately 50% more rainfall at site than we would normally expect,” while reporting impacts including “an abnormally high accumulation of water in the ponds on site” and “only minor damage to roads on site.”

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that the $44.85 million gross financing and quarter-end cash of $52.7 million were intended to fund an accelerated push on Mt. Todd permitting, technical optimization, and Australian team buildout, while keeping the company “with no debt.” The near-term catalyst focus centered on multiple permit modification steps (including approvals “expected early in Q3”) and technical work programs underway, while the longest-dated item remained the EPBC authorization targeted for “the middle or latter part of 2027,” aligning with management’s repeated expectation to begin detailed engineering and design in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. economy's nominal 'boom loop' - BofA
data.description:
The U.S. economy is experiencing an extraordinary nominal growth surge, with gross domestic product projected to climb roughly 75% from its pandemic low to 2027, according to Bank of America strategist Michael Hartnett.

The bank’s recent analysis shows U.S. nominal GDP rising from ~$20% in the second quarter of 2020 to a forecast $35T by the fourth quarter of 2027.

Hartnett described it as a “boom loop,” where higher prices, wages, government deficits, corporate revenues and rising asset values keep feeding into a larger dollar economy.

Advance GDP data arrived earlier in the week, with real Q1 2026 growth coming in at a firm, albeit softer-than-expected, 2.0%. Nominal growth grew at a much faster annualized rate of 5.6% in the same period, reflecting higher inflation.

data.title: Most and least shorted tech stocks with up to $2B market cap as of April
data.description:
Short interest in sub-$2B tech names is sharply bifurcated, with investors heavily betting against a concentrated group of AI-linked and semiconductor stocks while largely ignoring the rest of the small-cap universe.

The most shorted names, led by Xiao-I (AIXI) at nearly 66% and including C3.ai (AI), indie Semiconductor (INDI), and BigBear.ai (BBAI), are clustered in AI, semis, and structurally challenged tech, reflecting skepticism around valuations, execution, and cyclicality. In contrast, the least shorted stocks carry negligible short interest below 1%, signaling limited institutional positioning, lower liquidity, or simply a lack of conviction on either side.

Here’s a list of the 10 most shorted technology stocks (market cap up to $2B) as of April:

  • Xiao-I Corporation (AIXI): 65.78%
  • C3.ai (AI): 31.03%
  • indie Semiconductor (INDI): 30.28%
  • PAR Technology (PAR): 29.74%
  • BigBear.ai (BBAI): 25.17%
  • Wolfspeed (WOLF): 24.51%
  • Xerox (XRX): 24.38%
  • Canadian Solar (CSIQ): 21.44%
  • MicroVision (MVIS): 21.19%
  • Aeluma (ALMU): 20.46%

On the other end, the least shorted tech stocks (market cap up to $2B) include:

  • Taoping Inc. (TAOP): 0.51%
  • FatPipe, Inc. (FATN): 0.54%
  • Horizon Quantum Holdings Ltd. (HQ): 0.55%
  • InTest Corporation (INTT): 0.56%
  • SAIHEAT Limited (SAIH): 0.59%
  • BitFuFu Inc. (FUFU): 0.60%
  • NextTrip, Inc. (NTRP): 0.61%
  • Semilux International Ltd. (SELX): 0.62%
  • Sagtec Global Limited (SAGT): 0.63%
  • Trident Digital Tech Holdings Ltd. (TDTH): 0.64%

Key small- to mid-cap tech ETFs include Invesco S&P SmallCap Information Technology ETF (PSCT), SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD), ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), ARK Next Generation Internet ETF (ARKW), Global X Cloud Computing ETF (CLOU), Global X Cybersecurity ETF (BUG), Global X Robotics & AI ETF (BOTZ), Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth ETF (VOT), and iShares Russell Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IWP).

data.title: Seaport, Hemab price upsized IPOs eyeing $550M in proceeds
data.description:
Hemab Therapeutics (COAG), a drug developer targeting bleeding disorders, and neuropsychiatric drug developer Seaport Therapeutics (SPTX) have priced their upsized initial public offerings ahead of their trading debuts on Friday, seeking to raise more than $550M in total.

Cambridge, Massachusetts-based Hemab (COAG) priced its IPO offering of 16.75M shares of its common stock at $18.00 each, raising roughly $301.5M in gross proceeds. Underwriters will have a 30-day option to buy up to 2.5M additional shares.

The clinical-stage biotech previously sought to offer approximately 11.8M shares in the $16–$18 per share range.

Meanwhile, Seaport (SPTX), led by biotech veteran Daphne Zohar, priced its offering of nearly 14.2M shares at $18.00 each, expecting $254.9M in gross proceeds. 2.1M shares will be available additionally as part of a 30-day option to underwriters.

The Boston, Massachusetts-based firm previously sought to sell approximately 11.8M shares priced at $16 - $18 each. Both companies indicated a May 4 timeline to close their respective offerings.
